pattern: Only each number's gcd with k determines whether its product with another number clears k, so grouping numbers by that gcd shrinks a quadratic pair count down to a comparison over the (few) divisors of k.
hints: The only thing that matters about nums[i] for this problem is gcd(nums[i], k) -- two numbers with the same gcd against k behave identically when paired with anything else.
Group the array by gcd(value, k); the number of distinct groups is at most the number of divisors of k, which is small even when k is large.

For every pair of groups (including a group paired with itself), the pair qualifies for every combination of their members exactly when the product of the two groups' gcd values is itself divisible by k -- count group-pair combinations instead of element pairs, and use combinations-of-two within a group that pairs with itself.

edge_inputs.description: k equals one, every pair qualifies
every element individually divisible by k
no element alone is divisible by k, only certain combinations are
repeated equal values sharing one gcd group
values and k at the constraint ceiling

stress.description: random values and k, sized to keep the O(n^2) oracle fast
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 2000))
    nums = [rng.randint(1, 100000) for _ in range(n)]
    k = rng.randint(1, 100000)
    return [nums, k]
